intro.headline: Pressure-test how you think under real constraints
intro.body: The Deep Dive uses longer, scenario-based questions and targeted follow-up probes to build a more confident picture of how you reason, adapt, and execute across domains.

domains.label: Leadership
domains.description: Ability to align people, create clarity, and move decisions forward.

domains.label: Creativity
domains.description: Ability to generate novel approaches, ideas, and reframings.

domains.label: Strategy
domains.description: Ability to sequence action, model tradeoffs, and see leverage.

domains.label: Tech Proficiency
domains.description: Practical fluency with tools, systems, and technical workflows.

domains.label: Problem Solving
domains.description: Ability to decompose complexity and get to workable solutions.

domains.label: Critical Thinking
domains.description: Ability to question assumptions, weigh evidence, and reason rigorously.

domains.label: Adaptability
domains.description: Ability to adjust plans and behavior under changing conditions.

domains.label: Data Analysis
domains.description: Ability to use evidence, measurement, and quantitative signal.

archetypes.label: The Polymath
archetypes.description: A broad, balanced synthesizer with unusual pattern transfer across domains.

archetypes.label: The Strategist
archetypes.description: A systems-oriented thinker who links evidence, tradeoffs, and long-range direction.

archetypes.label: The Builder
archetypes.description: An execution-heavy profile that turns ideas into tools, systems, and outputs.

archetypes.label: The Leader
archetypes.description: A people-and-direction profile that creates clarity and adapts well under uncertainty.

core_questions.prompt: A cross-functional initiative is drifting because product, operations, and finance each define success differently. What is your first move?

options.label: Convene the key leads, define one outcome statement, and make tradeoffs explicit before assigning owners.
Interview each team separately to understand incentives, then tailor the plan to reduce resistance.
Model the operational bottlenecks first so the team debates constraints instead of opinions.
Draft a proposal yourself, circulate it asynchronously, and refine it once objections appear.

core_questions.prompt: Your team’s product has plateaued. Incremental improvements are no longer moving customer behavior. How do you open up better options?

options.label: Study analogous products in unrelated industries and import the structural ideas that could transfer.
Run a structured ideation sprint where constraints are deliberately reversed one by one.
Interview power users to uncover hidden workarounds and design from the behaviors already emerging.
Set measurable targets first and keep exploring only the ideas that can be defended with near-term evidence.

core_questions.prompt: Your team has three promising project directions but resources for only one. The data is incomplete and every choice creates second-order effects. What do you do?

options.label: Map second-order consequences, dependency chains, and risk-adjusted upside before sequencing the decision.
Run short experiments on all three options, then commit only once early evidence shifts the odds.
Prioritize the option that strengthens the team’s long-term capabilities, even if it is not the easiest short-term win.
Choose the option with the lowest implementation friction so you can build momentum and iterate later.

core_questions.prompt: A high-value workflow depends on several brittle manual steps between tools. You are not the primary engineer, but the work is clearly fragile. How do you respond?

options.label: Trace the data flow, identify the interfaces, and outline where automation or system handoffs would remove fragility.
Ask the engineers to explain the architecture and translate the business need into their language before proposing changes.
Measure failure points first so the team knows which technical problem is actually worth solving.
Prototype a low-risk workaround in existing tools so the team can learn before asking for deeper technical changes.

core_questions.prompt: A customer-facing problem has resurfaced three times despite previous fixes. What is your instinctive approach?

options.label: Reframe the issue as a system failure, identify the governing constraint, and test interventions at that point.
Break the problem into stages, isolate the exact failure mode, and rule out likely causes one by one.
Talk to the people closest to the issue, map where coordination breaks down, and redesign the process around handoffs.
Create a quick workaround first so the user pain drops immediately, then investigate root causes after stability returns.

core_questions.prompt: A senior stakeholder presents a confident recommendation backed by a polished deck. Several claims feel too neat. What do you do next?

options.label: Separate claims from evidence, test the assumptions underneath them, and ask what would change the conclusion.
Look for missing counterexamples and failure modes before deciding whether the recommendation is robust.
Ask for the raw evidence and rebuild the logic yourself to see whether the story holds under scrutiny.
Accept the proposal provisionally but design checkpoints so reality can challenge it quickly if needed.

core_questions.prompt: A major external change invalidates part of your quarterly plan two weeks before launch. What is your next move?

options.label: Preserve the core objective, cut nonessential scope, and re-sequence around the new constraints immediately.
Create fast alignment across the team so people know what changed, what did not, and where decisions now sit.
Run a rapid risk review, identify the most unstable assumptions, and redesign around the ones most likely to break next.
Build temporary workarounds so the team can keep shipping while you figure out the better long-term response.

core_questions.prompt: A dashboard shows a sharp performance increase after a recent product change. The team wants to declare success. What do you do?

options.label: Check whether the metric reflects the behavior you actually care about, then compare against a baseline and plausible confounders.
Break the uplift by segment and context before accepting the story the aggregate number suggests.
Treat the signal as promising but provisional, and design a cleaner experiment before rolling the change out wider.
Use the momentum to align stakeholders while you continue validating the signal in the background.

leadership.prompt: A strong individual contributor resists a team decision they did not help shape. Which response best matches you?

options.label: Acknowledge the concern, restate the decision logic, and clarify where dissent can still improve execution.
Invite them into a narrower decision they can own so resistance turns into responsibility.
Let the result decide; if the team sees the plan work, alignment will follow.
Escalate the issue quickly so the disagreement cannot slow momentum.

leadership.prompt: You need to delegate a workstream that matters but cannot receive much of your attention. What do you emphasize most?

options.label: Outcome, decision boundaries, and the earliest checkpoint where drift would still be cheap to fix.
The exact process you would follow so execution quality remains consistent.
The metrics you want reported back so you can intervene only when signal deteriorates.
The relationships they must manage so political friction does not surprise the team later.

creativity.prompt: You are asked for fresh ideas in a domain you do not know deeply. How do you avoid producing generic answers?

options.label: Translate the problem into its deeper structure, then borrow patterns from unrelated fields with the same structure.
Generate many options quickly, then use explicit criteria to filter them only after divergence is complete.
Ask domain experts for edge cases and build around the unusual constraints they reveal.
Look for whatever has worked before and add a small twist rather than risking a larger reframing.

creativity.prompt: A team says a constraint makes the goal impossible. What is the most creative next move?

options.label: Reframe the definition of success so the learning objective survives even if the original format cannot.
List which parts of the constraint are truly fixed and which only feel fixed because of habit.
Search for a partner, tool, or distribution model that changes the constraint instead of accepting it.
Accept the constraint and optimize within it; reframing usually costs too much time.

strategy.prompt: A decision looks attractive in the short term but could weaken an important capability later. How do you handle that tension?

options.label: Model the near-term gain against the capability cost and decide only after comparing second-order effects explicitly.
Choose the path that preserves option value, even if it looks slightly slower right now.
Take the short-term gain but add checkpoints to reverse course if the downstream cost grows.
Back the simpler move first; too much future-planning usually delays execution.

strategy.prompt: You inherit a chaotic portfolio of initiatives. Where do you begin?

options.label: Map dependencies, identify leverage points, and sequence the work so one move improves several others.
Cut low-value work first so the team regains attention and strategic decisions can improve afterward.
Stabilize one critical workstream end-to-end before trying to optimize the whole portfolio.
Ask each owner for their plan and aggregate the best pieces into a single roadmap.

tech_proficiency.prompt: A team uses the word "API" constantly and business decisions depend on it. What does strong non-engineering tech fluency look like here?

options.label: Understand what systems exchange, where the boundaries are, and how those interfaces constrain the business workflow.
Learn enough terminology to ask precise questions and avoid translating everything through a single engineer.
Focus on the customer outcome; technical details matter only if they surface as visible problems.
Build a simple diagram of the flow and use it to spot where handoffs, latency, or missing data create friction.

tech_proficiency.prompt: You suspect AI could remove repetitive work from a team. What is the best first step?

options.label: Document the workflow, inputs, outputs, and error patterns first so you know where AI could responsibly assist.
Prototype one narrow use case with clear review steps rather than proposing a broad transformation.
Wait for the tooling landscape to settle; premature experimentation usually creates noise.
Translate the workflow into technical requirements so specialists can tell you which parts are realistic to automate.

problem_solving.prompt: Two symptoms look unrelated, but you suspect they share a root cause. What is your approach?

options.label: Map the system, test where the flows intersect, and look for one constraint capable of producing both symptoms.
Trace both issues separately until they converge on the same failure mechanism or prove they do not.
Interview the operators closest to the work because they often know where the hidden coupling lives.
Fix the more painful symptom first; common causes can be investigated later if the second issue persists.

problem_solving.prompt: A proposed solution works in theory but keeps failing in practice. Where do you look first?

options.label: At the assumptions about the real environment, because practical failure usually means the problem framing was incomplete.
At the interfaces and handoffs, because implementation breaks where ownership or context is lost.
At whether the team explored enough alternatives before locking onto this solution path.
At whether the rollout moved too slowly and lost momentum rather than whether the design itself was weak.

critical_thinking.prompt: A metric supports your preferred conclusion, but the sample is small and the timing is suspicious. How do you think about it?

options.label: Treat it as weak evidence, ask what else could explain the result, and avoid strong claims until the signal stabilizes.
Use it as one input but seek disconfirming evidence before letting it drive a decision.
Take the evidence as directionally useful if the downside of acting is small.
If it matches your prior belief and the team needs momentum, it is probably enough to move forward.

critical_thinking.prompt: You and a colleague interpret the same evidence differently. What helps you resolve the disagreement best?

options.label: Surface the hidden assumptions each of you is using and identify which one the evidence actually speaks to.
Define what evidence would change each position; if none would, the disagreement is not really about evidence.
Time-box the debate and run the cheapest test that could reduce uncertainty.
Defer to whichever interpretation comes from the more experienced person.

adaptability.prompt: Halfway through a plan, new information invalidates your strongest assumption. What is the healthiest reaction?

options.label: Update the model quickly, preserve the underlying objective, and explain the change clearly so the team can pivot without confusion.
Pause briefly, identify which decisions are now wrong, and restart only the parts of the plan affected by the new signal.
Keep the current plan alive while collecting more evidence; fast pivots can also create avoidable chaos.
Commit harder to the original plan unless the new information becomes overwhelming.

adaptability.prompt: You are moving between several contexts in one day and quality is slipping. What adjustment is most like you?

options.label: Reduce context switching by batching similar decisions and building deliberate reset points between modes of work.
Create lightweight checklists so each context has a faster re-entry ramp and less cognitive residue.
Accept the volatility and rely on improvisation; too much structure makes change harder.
Hand off the noisiest work temporarily so you can stabilize the most fragile commitments first.

data_analysis.prompt: A team keeps citing one headline metric, but you suspect it hides the real dynamics. What do you do?

options.label: Break the metric into components and segments until you can see which behavior is actually driving the aggregate.
Ask whether the metric is a good proxy at all before spending more time analyzing it.
Look for leading indicators around the metric so you can detect change before the headline number moves.
Use the headline metric for alignment and worry about deeper analysis only if the trend reverses.

data_analysis.prompt: You can only run one small experiment before making a larger decision. What distinguishes a strong experiment design?

options.label: It isolates a meaningful variable, defines the decision threshold in advance, and uses a metric tied to the real outcome.
It is cheap, fast, and directional enough to update the next decision even if it is not perfect science.
It gets stakeholder buy-in first so everyone trusts the conclusion when the numbers arrive.
It produces a number the team can point to, even if the exact variable being tested is still messy.

scenarios.title: AI Workflow Rollout
scenarios.context: Your organization wants to introduce AI support into a critical operations workflow. Leaders want speed, operators worry about quality drift, and the underlying process is not documented well enough for easy automation.

questions.prompt: What is the highest-leverage first step?

options.label: Document the workflow, error patterns, and review checkpoints before introducing any AI-assisted step.
Pilot one narrow use case with clear human review and use the result to build credibility for expansion.
Start with the loudest stakeholder concerns so the rollout does not get blocked politically.
Wait until the tooling is more mature; operational workflows are too sensitive for early experimentation.

questions.prompt: The pilot saves time, but error rates rise slightly. How do you interpret the result?

options.label: Treat it as a promising but incomplete result and redesign the pilot around quality thresholds before expanding.
Keep the pilot running but narrow the use case until the quality problem is constrained.
Share the mixed result transparently and align on the tradeoff the organization is willing to make.
If the time savings are large enough, push forward and fix quality later through training.

scenarios.title: New Market Entry
scenarios.context: A company sees signs of demand in a new market, but the signal is patchy. Sales wants to move fast, finance wants tighter evidence, and product knows the current offering is not a clean fit.

questions.prompt: How would you make the first decision?

options.label: Design a staged entry with clear learning goals so each step buys better evidence for the next.
Interview prospective customers deeply to understand where the offering fits and where it breaks.
Map the second-order consequences of entry, including distraction cost and capability gains, before committing.
Send a small team immediately; imperfect information is normal and action reveals more than planning.

questions.prompt: Early customer conversations are positive, but product usage data stays weak. What do you conclude?

options.label: Interest is not the same as adoption; find the activation constraint before expanding investment.
The value proposition is probably mispositioned; reframe the offer around the strongest use case emerging in interviews.
Bring sales, product, and finance into one review so the organization can interpret the mixed signal together.
Usage data lags; keep pushing acquisition and wait for behavior to catch up.

scenarios.title: Reorganization Under Pressure
scenarios.context: A reorganization merges two teams with different cultures, tools, and performance histories. Morale is uneven, accountability is fuzzy, and key people might leave if the transition feels chaotic.

options.label: Clarify the new operating model, decision rights, and what success now looks like before redesigning everything else.
Map where the teams overlap, where they conflict, and which interfaces are most likely to break first.
Find the few people whose trust the rest of the organization will follow and win them early.
Preserve existing workflows for a while; too much change at once is the bigger risk.

questions.prompt: Two months in, metrics are flat but qualitative feedback says the merged team is clearer and faster. How do you respond?

options.label: Treat the qualitative signal as useful but temporary; identify leading indicators that should move before core metrics do.
Keep reinforcing the new operating rhythm while checking whether measurement lag is hiding real progress.
Run a focused diagnostic on the bottleneck that still prevents the clarity gains from turning into output gains.
If morale improved, assume performance will eventually follow and avoid over-analyzing the lag.

scenarios.title: Data Platform Migration
scenarios.context: A company must migrate to a new data platform before a contract deadline. The old system is unstable, the new system is only partially configured, and every shortcut risks compounding future maintenance costs.

questions.prompt: How do you structure the migration plan?

options.label: Sequence the migration by dependency and business criticality so the riskiest interfaces are stabilized first.
Define a minimal viable migration, keep the architecture legible, and avoid shortcuts that create hidden debt.
Create a shared command center so engineering, analytics, and business stakeholders can surface issues quickly.
Move the easiest workloads first to show progress and reduce anxiety before tackling harder dependencies.

questions.prompt: Mid-migration, one team wants to skip validation to hit the deadline. What do you do?

options.label: Make the risk explicit, define what validation cannot be skipped, and re-scope lower-value work instead.
Negotiate a narrower validated release that preserves trust in the migration while keeping the deadline in sight.
Use the existing defect data to show which skipped checks create the highest downstream cost.
Accept the shortcut temporarily; reliability can be improved after the contract risk is gone.
